source: OET public Speaking assessment criteria (from September 2018)

bands.6: Pronunciation is easily understood and prosodic features (stress, intonation, rhythm) are used effectively. L1 accent has no effect on intelligibility.
bands.5: Easily understood. Communication is not impeded by a few pronunciation or prosodic errors and/or noticeable L1 accent. Minimal strain for the listener.
bands.4: Easily understood most of the time. Pronunciation or prosodic errors and/or L1 accent at times cause strain for the listener.
bands.3: Produces some acceptable features of spoken English. Difficult to understand because errors in pronunciation/stress/intonation and/or L1 accent cause serious strain.
bands.2: Often unintelligible. Frequent errors in pronunciation/stress/intonation and/or L1 accent cause severe strain for the listener.
bands.0: Candidate does not provide any response.

bands.6: Completely fluent speech at normal speed. Any hesitation is appropriate and not a sign of searching for words or structures.
bands.5: Fluent speech at normal speed, with only occasional repetition or self-correction. Hesitation may occasionally indicate searching but is generally appropriate.
bands.4: Uneven flow, with some repetition, especially in longer utterances. Some evidence of searching for words. Delivery may be staccato or too fast/slow.
bands.3: Very uneven. Frequent pauses and repetitions indicate searching. Excessive use of fillers and difficulty sustaining longer utterances cause serious strain.
bands.2: Extremely uneven. Long pauses, numerous repetition and self-corrections make speech difficult to follow.
bands.1: Impossible to follow, consisting of isolated words and phrases and self-corrections, separated by long pauses.
bands.0: Candidate does not provide any response.

bands.6: Entirely appropriate register, tone and lexis for the context. No difficulty at all in explaining technical matters in lay terms.
bands.5: Mostly appropriate register, tone and lexis. Occasional lapses are not intrusive.
bands.4: Generally appropriate but somewhat restricted and lacking in complexity. Lapses are noticeable and at times reflect limited resources.
bands.3: Some evidence of appropriate register, tone and lexis, but lapses are frequent and intrusive.
bands.2: Mostly inappropriate register, tone and lexis for the context.
bands.1: Entirely inappropriate register, tone and lexis for the context.
bands.0: Candidate does not provide any response.

bands.6: Rich and flexible. Wide range of grammar and vocabulary used accurately and flexibly. Confident use of idiomatic speech.
bands.5: Wide range of grammar and vocabulary generally used accurately and flexibly. Occasional errors are not intrusive.
bands.4: Sufficient resources to maintain the interaction. Inaccuracies in vocabulary and grammar, particularly in more complex sentences, are sometimes intrusive. Meaning is generally clear.
bands.3: Limited vocabulary and control of grammatical structures, except very simple sentences. Persistent inaccuracies are intrusive.
bands.2: Very limited resources of vocabulary and grammar, even in simple sentences. Numerous errors in word choice.
bands.0: Candidate does not provide any response.

indicators.text: Initiating the interaction appropriately (greeting, introductions, nature of interview)
Demonstrating an attentive and respectful attitude
Adopting a non-judgemental approach
Showing empathy for feelings/predicament/emotional state
Eliciting and exploring the patient's ideas/concerns/expectations
Picking up the patient's cues
Relating explanations to elicited ideas/concerns/expectations
Sequencing the interview purposefully and logically
Signposting changes in topic
Using organising techniques in explanations
Facilitating the patient's narrative with active listening techniques, minimising interruption
Using initially open questions, appropriately moving to closed questions
NOT using compound questions/leading questions
Clarifying statements which are vague or need amplification
Summarising information to encourage correction/invite further information
Establishing initially what the patient already knows
Pausing periodically when giving information, using the response to guide next steps
Encouraging the patient to contribute reactions/feelings
Checking whether the patient has understood information
Discovering what further information the patient needs
